name: Cleveland-Cliffs Inc.

profile.description: Cleveland-Cliffs is the largest flat-rolled steel producer in North America, with approximately 30 million net tons of annual steelmaking capacity and $18.9B in TTM revenue. Through four major acquisitions under CEO Lourenco Goncalves (AK Steel, ArcelorMittal USA, Ferrous Processing & Trading, and Stelco), the company transformed from an iron ore miner into a vertically integrated steel and mining company serving automotive, infrastructure, and manufacturing markets. Headquartered in Cleveland, Ohio with approximately 25,000 employees.

innovation.label: DEVELOPING
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): Cleveland-Cliffs executed a transformative series of four acquisitions from 2020-2024, reinventing itself from an iron ore miner into North America's largest flat-rolled steel producer — a fundamental business model transformation [1]. The Stelco acquisition expanded Canadian operations and the company's Hot Briquetted Iron (HBI) production represents genuinely differentiated manufacturing capability. AI Deployment (1/2): The company announced a multi-year strategic partnership with Palantir Technologies in April 2026, deploying Foundry and AIP across manufacturing operations for production planning, order entry, and real-time coordination. CEO Goncalves called it 'nothing short of a gamechanger' for the complexity of integrated steelmaking [3]. No quantified revenue impact yet. Market Share (1/2): Largest flat-rolled steel producer in North America and GM Supplier of the Year, with dominant positions in automotive-grade steel. However, share is maintained through consolidation rather than organic growth [1]. Product Pipeline (1/2): The slab contract termination provides a $500M EBITDA tailwind, and management guides to positive free cash flow starting Q2 2026 with strong FCF in H2. The company is selling eight non-EBITDA-contributing assets for ~$425M to fund debt reduction [1]. R&D Intensity (0/1): R&D spending is not separately disclosed and is assumed modest relative to revenue for a steel manufacturer.

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (3/4): Cleveland-Cliffs' mission to be 'the most profitable, environmentally and socially responsible, and customer-preferred supplier of iron ore and steel products' combined with Goncalves's passionate advocacy for American steel and domestic manufacturing jobs reflects a specific and thoughtful purpose [4]. The cause — rebuilding and protecting American steel manufacturing — is clearly connected to community economic health and national supply chain security. It has been consistently pursued through a decade of acquisitions and trade policy advocacy. Community Impact (1/2): Cleveland-Cliffs is the largest employer in multiple steel towns across the Midwest and Great Lakes region, supporting local communities in Cleveland, Pittsburgh, and smaller manufacturing cities. The company's 25,000 jobs in historically disadvantaged manufacturing regions represent meaningful community impact [4]. Employee Retention (1/2): Goncalves has maintained a core leadership team through multiple major acquisitions, and the company has successfully integrated four acquisitions without catastrophic cultural failures. Union workforce with structured labor relations provides stability, though no specific retention data is available. Insider Buying (1/2): CEO Goncalves maintains a significant equity stake, though his trust sold 3 million shares ($37.2M) in February 2026 and CFO Celso Goncalves Jr. sold 214K shares ($2.9M) in June 2026 [5]. Net insider selling pattern suggests limited conviction at current prices.

infrastructure.label: DEVELOPING
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on available metrics with 5-year revenue CAGR excluded. Q1 2026 revenues of $4.9B (up 6.3% YoY) beat the $4.81B consensus, and the adjusted loss narrowed sharply to $0.40/share from $0.93/share a year earlier [1]. D/E of 129.3% is elevated from acquisition financing but improving through the $425M asset sale program and commitment to use 100% of operating cash flow for debt reduction [1]. ROE is deeply negative at -18.6% due to net losses, scoring at floor. Revenue growth YoY of 6.3% is positive but below the 10% target. Forward P/E of 28.9 reflects the early-cycle recovery priced into the stock. Profit margin of -6.4% is negative, scoring at floor. PEG of 0.43 is highly favorable, scoring at maximum, reflecting the expected earnings recovery from current depressed levels [1]. The $500M slab contract termination EBITDA tailwind and debt reduction plan provide a credible path to profitability improvement.

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (7/9): Steel manufacturing is fundamentally a physical-world, capital-intensive industry where AI cannot displace the core activity of converting iron ore into steel via blast furnaces or electric arc furnaces [1]. Cleveland-Cliffs' vertically integrated model — from iron ore mining through HBI production through steelmaking through finishing — requires massive physical infrastructure, specialized metallurgical expertise, and logistics coordination that AI structurally cannot substitute. AI may actually increase demand for steel through data center construction (AI infrastructure requires steel-framed buildings) and infrastructure investment. Analysts do not cite AI as a displacement risk for Cleveland-Cliffs. AI Opportunity (1/1): The Palantir strategic partnership deploys AI across Cleveland-Cliffs' manufacturing footprint for production planning, order entry, and real-time operational coordination [3]. CEO Goncalves has described the initial pilot results as transformative for managing the complexity of integrated steelmaking. While not generating AI revenue externally, this represents meaningful AI-driven efficiency gains in a 25,000-employee, $19B-revenue operation.

infinite_game.summary: CEO Goncalves has demonstrated extraordinary courage and existential flexibility in transforming Cleveland-Cliffs from an iron ore miner into North America's largest flat-rolled steel producer through four major acquisitions in four years. His decade-long advocacy for American steel and manufacturing jobs reflects a genuine purpose. However, his combative competitive style, significant insider selling, and the tension between his bold vision and the company's current financial challenges create a mixed infinite game profile.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): Goncalves's cause — rebuilding American steel manufacturing to ensure domestic supply chain security and preserve manufacturing jobs in communities that depend on them — is genuine, service-oriented, and has been consistently pursued through a decade of bold acquisitions and trade policy advocacy [4]. It is specific and distinctive from peers. However, the cause is partially political in framing and has not survived a leadership transition. Goncalves IS the cause — it's unclear whether Cleveland-Cliffs' purpose would endure under a successor.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): Goncalves has maintained a core leadership team through four major acquisitions, successfully integrating diverse workforces from AK Steel, ArcelorMittal USA, Ferrous Processing, and Stelco without cultural collapse [1]. The 25,000-employee, heavily unionized workforce operates under structured labor agreements that provide stability. However, Goncalves's forceful personality and combative public style may limit psychological safety at the executive level. No specific employee retention or satisfaction data available.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (0/1): Goncalves's public language toward US Steel and foreign steel producers is combative — focused on winning market share and blocking competition through tariffs rather than learning from rivals [4]. His aggressive pursuit of US Steel and public criticism of Nippon Steel's acquisition bid reflects finite competitive thinking. The competitive strategy is about consolidation and market control rather than self-improvement through competitive inspiration.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (2/2): The transformation from a niche iron ore mining company into North America's largest flat-rolled steel producer — through four acquisitions totaling billions of dollars between 2020 and 2024 — is a textbook example of existential flexibility [1]. Goncalves fundamentally reinvented the company, abandoning the iron ore commodity business model to build a vertically integrated steel platform serving automotive OEMs. The HBI production capability further differentiates the company from conventional steelmakers. This was transformative, not incremental.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): Goncalves's four acquisitions in four years — including ArcelorMittal USA for $1.4B during COVID-19 — required extraordinary financial courage [1]. Maintaining capital investment through the brutal 2025 steel downcycle ($1.4B net loss, $37M EBITDA) while committing to debt reduction and the Palantir AI partnership demonstrates long-term conviction. However, the magnitude of the 2025 financial miss raises questions about whether the acquisitive strategy was prudent given the leverage it created.

investment_thesis: Cleveland-Cliffs is a turnaround story with identifiable 2026 catalysts. Q1 2026 revenue of $4.9B (up 6.3% YoY) beat consensus, and the adjusted loss narrowed sharply to $0.40/share from $0.93/share a year earlier [1]. The slab contract termination delivers an estimated $500M annual EBITDA tailwind, $425M in non-core asset sales funds debt reduction, and management guides to positive free cash flow starting Q2 2026 with strong FCF in H2 [1]. The Palantir AI partnership promises operational efficiency gains across the manufacturing footprint [3]. At PEG of 0.43 and GM Supplier of the Year status, the stock offers compelling cyclical upside if steel pricing stabilizes and automotive demand recovers. U.S. tariff protections provide a pricing floor for domestic flat-rolled steel.
thesis_continued: The bear case is that 2025 financial performance was catastrophic — $1.4B GAAP net loss and $37M Adjusted EBITDA — making the 2026 recovery dependent on every identified catalyst materializing [1]. D/E of 129.3% leaves limited balance sheet flexibility if recovery lags, and the commitment to use 100% of operating cash flow for debt reduction means no dividend reinstatement or share buybacks in the near term. Trailing ROE of -18.6% and profit margin of -6.4% reflect a business that has not yet demonstrated sustainable profitability post-acquisitions. CEO Goncalves's trust sold $37.2M in shares at depressed prices, and CFO sales followed — unusual insider selling during what management describes as a turnaround [5]. Chinese steel overcapacity and global pricing competition threaten domestic pricing power despite tariffs, and the EV transition creates uncertainty around traditional automotive steel demand.
key_catalysts: Slab contract termination delivering estimated $500M annual EBITDA benefit beginning in 2026, the single largest financial improvement lever, validated by Q1 loss narrowing from $0.93 to $0.40/share
Palantir AI partnership driving measurable operational efficiency gains across 25,000-employee manufacturing footprint, potentially reducing costs in production planning and quality optimization
U.S. steel tariff protections and GM Supplier of the Year status providing pricing support and customer relationship stability as automotive production recovers
key_risks: D/E of 129.3% and $1.4B 2025 net loss create financial fragility — if the 2026 recovery is slower than guided, debt covenants or refinancing risk could materially impair equity value
Insider selling by CEO Goncalves ($37.2M) and CFO ($2.9M) during a declared turnaround period signals potential misalignment between management's public optimism and private conviction

Automotive production remaining weak through 2026 as OEMs manage EV transition inventory — Cleveland-Cliffs derives ~30% of revenues from automotive, and electric vehicles require less flat-rolled steel than ICE vehicles
